name: Vetala
description: A Vetala is a mischievous and malevolent spirit in Hindu mythology, often described as a reanimated corpse possessed by a spirit. They inhabit cemeteries and cremation grounds, feeding on the decaying flesh and preying on the living. Vetalas are capable of possessing corpses and using them as puppets, animating them with supernatural strength and agility. They are known for their cunning and trickery, posing riddles and challenges to travelers, and inflicting misfortune upon those who fail to answer correctly. They are often associated with black magic and necromancy, and are feared for their ability to manipulate the natural world and cause chaos. Vetalas are not inherently immortal but can prolong their existence by possessing new corpses. They are considered to be powerful adversaries, requiring skilled exorcists or learned individuals with profound knowledge of Vedic scriptures to subdue or control them. Their power lies in their ability to exploit the vulnerabilities and fears of humans, making them formidable opponents in both the physical and spiritual realms. They are also known to guard treasures and secrets, only revealing them to those who prove worthy through trials of intellect and courage. The Vetala's existence underscores the Hindu belief in the interconnectedness of life and death, and the potential for spirits to linger in the mortal world.
subtitle: Corpse-possessing mischievous spirit
appearance: Vetalas are typically described as appearing as reanimated corpses, often grotesque and decaying. Their skin is pale and clammy, and their eyes are bloodshot and filled with malice. They may exhibit signs of decomposition, such as rotting flesh and exposed bones. In some depictions, they can alter their appearance to appear more human-like, albeit with an unsettling aura. The Vetala's physical form is largely dependent on the state of the corpse they inhabit, but their presence is always marked by an unsettling and unnatural feeling.
habitat: Cemeteries, cremation grounds, abandoned places, and desolate forests.
behavior: Vetalas are generally malevolent and mischievous. They enjoy tormenting humans by posing riddles, inflicting curses, and causing misfortune. They are cunning and manipulative, often using their powers of illusion to deceive and trick their victims. They are fiercely protective of their territory and will attack anyone who trespasses upon it. While primarily malevolent, some vetalas can be controlled or even befriended by powerful sorcerers or individuals who demonstrate intelligence and courage.

keyMyths.title: Baital Pachisi (Vikram and the Vampire)
keyMyths.description: The most famous Vetala story involves King Vikramaditya carrying a Vetala on his shoulder. The Vetala tells the king stories and poses riddles, and if Vikramaditya speaks, the Vetala will return to its tree.

sources.author: Somadeva
sources.description: A collection of Indian folk tales, including stories featuring Vetalas.
sources.text: A collection of Indian folk tales, including stories featuring Vetalas.

sources.author: Various
sources.description: A collection of twenty-five stories featuring a Vetala and King Vikramaditya.
